edges.description: Sustained hyperuricemia drives serum urate beyond its saturation point, promoting nucleation and deposition of monosodium urate crystals in joints and soft tissues.

Sustained hyperuricemia and hyperuricosuria, together with low urinary pH, promote uric acid supersaturation and crystallization in the renal collecting system, producing uric acid nephrolithiasis. This renal stone pathway is mechanistically distinct from articular monosodium urate deposition.
intermediate_mechanisms: acidic urinary pH promoting uric acid crystallization

edges.description: Deposited MSU crystals are phagocytosed by resident macrophages, triggering NLRP3 inflammasome activation and the acute inflammatory cascade.
IL-1beta and downstream cytokines/chemokines released after inflammasome activation recruit neutrophils to the joint, amplifying acute inflammation.
The inflammatory milieu around MSU crystals engages the complement system, with C5 among the key emergent inflammatory mediators.
Macrophage NLRP3 inflammasome activation initiates the acute gouty inflammatory flare manifesting as acute arthritis.
Recruited neutrophils exposed to MSU crystals form neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) in the inflamed joint.
Neutrophil influx and amplified inflammation produce the intense joint pain of the acute gout attack.
Neutrophil-amplified joint inflammation produces the swelling characteristic of the acute gout attack.
Complement activation, including C5/C5a generation, contributes to neutrophil recruitment and amplification of the inflammatory response.
MSU-induced NETs initially exacerbate the acute inflammatory flare before aggregated NETs drive its self-limited resolution.

Higher serum uric acid tracks the hyperuricemic biochemical state that drives urate crystal supersaturation; reduction under xanthine oxidase inhibitors, URAT1 inhibitors, or uricase reports pharmacodynamic urate-lowering in gout.

evidence.reference_title: Unraveling the pathological biomineralization of monosodium urate crystals in gout patients.
evidence.snippet: Crystallization of monosodium urate monohydrate (MSU) leads to painful gouty arthritis.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanism of macrophages in gout: Recent progress and perspective.
evidence.snippet: Macrophages assume a pivotal role in the initiation of acute gouty inflammation and subsequent inflammatory cascades.

nodes.description: MSU crystals activate the complement system, particularly C5, contributing to neutrophil recruitment and amplifying the inflammatory response. Effective urate-lowering therapy modulates complement and inflammatory protein networks.

evidence.reference_title: Effective xanthine oxidase inhibitor urate lowering therapy in gout is linked to an emergent serum protein interactome of complement and inflammation modulators.
evidence.snippet: Sera demonstrated altered complement activation and regulatory gene ontology biologic processes.

evidence.reference_title: Effective xanthine oxidase inhibitor urate lowering therapy in gout is linked to an emergent serum protein interactome of complement and inflammation modulators.
evidence.snippet: Reduced gout flares are linked with a XOI treatment-emergent serum protein interactome that includes inflammation regulators, associated with altered complement activation and regulatory biologic processes.

evidence.reference_title: Effective xanthine oxidase inhibitor urate lowering therapy in gout is linked to an emergent serum protein interactome of complement and inflammation modulators.
evidence.snippet: Last, febuxostat treatment decreased complement activation biologic process proteins in cultured BMDMs.

nodes.description: Monosodium urate (MSU) crystals deposit in joints and soft tissues when serum urate exceeds saturation point (~6.8 mg/dL). Crystals trigger inflammatory response.

evidence.reference_title: Unraveling the pathological biomineralization of monosodium urate crystals in gout patients.
evidence.snippet: inflammatory MSU crystals form after a non-inflammatory amorphous precursor (AMSU) that nucleates heterogeneously on collagen fibrils from damaged articular cartilage of gout patients

evidence.reference_title: Unraveling the pathological biomineralization of monosodium urate crystals in gout patients.
evidence.snippet: This non-classical crystallization route imprints a nanogranular structure to biogenic acicular MSU crystals, which have smaller unit cell volume, lower microstrain, and higher crystallinity than synthetic MSU.

nodes.description: Elevated serum uric acid from increased purine metabolism or decreased renal excretion leads to supersaturation and crystal formation. Urate is the end product of purine catabolism in humans.

evidence.reference_title: Unraveling the pathological biomineralization of monosodium urate crystals in gout patients.
evidence.snippet: Crystallization of monosodium urate monohydrate (MSU) leads to painful gouty arthritis.

nodes.description: MSU crystals are phagocytosed by macrophages, activating NLRP3 inflammasome and triggering IL-1beta release, driving acute inflammatory flare.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanism of macrophages in gout: Recent progress and perspective.
evidence.snippet: Gout represents an autoinflammatory disorder instigated by monosodium urate crystals. Its primary manifestation involves the recruitment of diverse immune cell populations, including neutrophils and macrophages. Macrophages assume a pivotal role in the initiation of acute gouty inflammation and subsequent inflammatory cascades.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanism of macrophages in gout: Recent progress and perspective.
evidence.snippet: Macrophages, characterized by different subtypes, exhibit distinct functionalities that either contribute to the progression or regression of gout.

evidence.reference_title: Effective xanthine oxidase inhibitor urate lowering therapy in gout is linked to an emergent serum protein interactome of complement and inflammation modulators.
evidence.snippet: At 48 weeks ULT, serum urate normalized in all gout patients, and flares declined in association with significantly altered proteins (p < 0.05) in clustering and proteome networks in sera and peripheral blood mononuclear cells.

nodes.description: Neutrophils form extracellular traps (NETs) in response to MSU crystals. NETs initially amplify inflammation but aggregated NETs (aggNETs) promote resolution by encapsulating crystals, degrading cytokines, and blocking further neutrophil recruitment. NETs also contribute to tophi formation.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanism of neutrophil extracellular traps in the pathogenesis of gout.
evidence.snippet: Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) are formed by neutrophils in response to pathogen attack. During gout, NETs induced by MSU crystals exacerbate inflammation, and aggregated NETs (aggNETs) promote the resolution of gout-associated inflammation by encapsulating MSU crystals, degrading cytokines and chemokines, and blocking the recruitment and activation of neutrophils.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanism of neutrophil extracellular traps in the pathogenesis of gout.
evidence.snippet: With disease progression, NETs participate in the formation of tophi.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanism of neutrophil extracellular traps in the pathogenesis of gout.
evidence.snippet: Therefore, aggNETs are a possible mechanism of spontaneous gout regression.

nodes.description: IL-1beta and other cytokines recruit neutrophils to the joint, amplifying inflammation and causing the intense pain and swelling of acute gout attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanism of macrophages in gout: Recent progress and perspective.
evidence.snippet: Its primary manifestation involves the recruitment of diverse immune cell populations, including neutrophils and macrophages.

evidence.reference_title: Effective xanthine oxidase inhibitor urate lowering therapy in gout is linked to an emergent serum protein interactome of complement and inflammation modulators.
evidence.snippet: In both cohorts, a treatment-emergent serum interactome included key gouty inflammation mediators (C5, IL-1B, CXCL8, IL6).

readouts.interpretation: Higher serum uric acid tracks the hyperuricemic biochemical state that drives urate crystal supersaturation; reduction under xanthine oxidase inhibitors, URAT1 inhibitors, or uricase reports pharmacodynamic urate-lowering in gout.
